name: Agile & Scrum
blurb: Sprints, ceremonies, user stories, velocity, Kanban.

lessons.title: Agile vs Waterfall & the Agile Manifesto
lessons.concept:
Imagine building a wedding hall the old way — the "Waterfall" way. You finalise every detail on paper first, then design, then construct, then inspect, then hand over the keys. Each phase finishes fully before the next begins, like water falling down steps that it can never flow back up. This worked for construction, and India's early IT-services giants built entire delivery models on it: gather every requirement into a giant document, disappear for a year, and deliver at the very end. The trouble is that software is not a building. By the time you deliver, the customer's business has moved on and that 300-page spec is already stale.

In February 2001, seventeen software developers met at Snowbird, Utah and wrote the Agile Manifesto — a concise 68-word statement built on four values. They value individuals and interactions over processes and tools; working software over comprehensive documentation; customer collaboration over contract negotiation; and responding to change over following a plan. Crucially they added that the items on the right still have value, but the items on the left are valued MORE. Agile is not "no planning" or "no documentation" — it is delivering working software in small, frequent slices and welcoming change instead of fearing it. The Manifesto is also backed by twelve supporting principles, which spell out ideas like early and continuous delivery and sustainable pace.

Walk into any Indian product company today — Flipkart, Zomato, Cars24, PhonePe — and you will hear "sprint," "standup," and "backlog." The shift from services-era Waterfall to product-era Agile is one of the biggest changes in Indian tech careers. For a data analyst the difference is felt daily: instead of a fixed six-month "build all the dashboards" project, you ship one useful dashboard every two weeks, gather feedback, and adjust. This mindset is the foundation the rest of this track builds on.
lessons.example: A Waterfall used-car pricing project might spend three months writing the "perfect" spec and five months building, only to launch a model the market has already outgrown. An Agile team ships a rough pricing dashboard in two weeks, learns that dealers care most about one city's live inventory, and pivots — delivering real value while the Waterfall team is still polishing documentation.
lessons.code:
THE FOUR VALUES (valued item is always on the LEFT):
  Individuals & interactions   >  processes & tools
  Working software             >  comprehensive documentation
  Customer collaboration       >  contract negotiation
  Responding to change         >  following a plan

QUICK HEURISTIC — Agile or Waterfall?
  Fixed scope locked up front, sequential phases,
     change discouraged .......................... Waterfall
  Small frequent deliveries, feedback loops,
     change welcomed ............................. Agile
lessons.tryit: Think of your current or most recent project. Write down: (1) Was it run Waterfall or Agile? (2) Name one specific place where "responding to change over following a plan" would have saved time or rework. Bring that example to your next team discussion.

mistakes.m: Thinking Agile means no planning and no documentation.
mistakes.fix: Agile values working software MORE than comprehensive documentation — not instead of it. Teams still plan and document; they just do it in short cycles and keep docs lightweight and current.

mistakes.m: Believing Waterfall is always wrong and Agile always right.
mistakes.fix: Waterfall still suits fixed-scope, high-certainty work such as a regulatory report with legally fixed requirements. Choose based on how much change and uncertainty you actually expect.

mistakes.m: Treating Agile as a tool you install, like Jira.
mistakes.fix: Jira is only a tool. Agile is the four values and twelve principles — you can be "using Jira" while completely ignoring customer collaboration and welcoming change.

quiz.q: According to the Agile Manifesto, which pairing is stated correctly (the more-valued item first)?
opts: Comprehensive documentation over working software
Working software over comprehensive documentation
Following a plan over responding to change
Processes and tools over individuals and interactions
quiz.why: In all four value statements the more-valued item sits on the left. "Working software over comprehensive documentation" is one of them; the other three options reverse the wording, which makes them wrong.
quiz.approach: Recall the four value pairs and remember the valued item is always on the LEFT. Any option that flips the order (documentation first, plan first, processes first) is a distractor.

quiz.q: Which is a hallmark of Waterfall compared to Agile?
opts: Delivering working software every two weeks
Welcoming changing requirements late in development
Completing each phase fully before the next begins, with change discouraged
Daily collaboration with the customer
quiz.why: Waterfall runs sequential phases (requirements to design to build to test to release) where each finishes before the next starts, making late change expensive and discouraged. The other options describe Agile.
quiz.approach: Ask whether the option embraces frequent delivery and change (Agile) or locked sequential phases (Waterfall). Only the sequential-phases option fits Waterfall.

quiz.q: Which statement best reflects the Agile mindset for a data analyst?
opts: I'll gather every possible requirement, then disappear for six months to build all dashboards.
Documentation is banned; we just start writing queries.
I'll ship one useful dashboard, get feedback, and refine it next sprint.
Once the plan is signed off we never change scope.
quiz.why: Agile favours small, frequent, value-delivering increments with feedback loops. The first and last options are Waterfall thinking; the second misreads Agile as "no documentation," which is false.
quiz.approach: Eliminate options that lock scope or claim Agile bans documentation. The remaining one — iterative delivery with feedback — is the Agile way.

lessons.title: The Scrum Framework: Roles, Events & Artifacts
lessons.concept:
If Agile is the philosophy, Scrum is the most popular way to actually do it — used by a large majority of Agile teams in India, from startups to global capability centres. Scrum organises work into short, fixed cycles called Sprints, usually two weeks long. Think of it like a web series: instead of releasing one giant film after two years, you ship a watchable episode every fortnight and learn from viewers each time. The 2020 Scrum Guide defines just three accountabilities (commonly called roles), five events, and three artifacts — deliberately lightweight.

The three roles. The Product Owner owns the "what and why": they order the Product Backlog to maximise the product's value and act as the voice of the customer and business. The Scrum Master owns "how well the team works": the 2020 Scrum Guide calls them a "true leader who serves" the team and the wider organisation — a coach and facilitator (the earlier idea of a servant-leader) who helps the team apply Scrum, removes blockers, and shields it from distractions. They are NOT the boss who assigns tasks. The Developers own the "how": they build the increment. In Scrum, "Developers" means everyone doing the work — data analysts, testers, and designers included, not only coders. A whole Scrum Team is typically ten people or fewer.

The events and artifacts. The Sprint is the container that holds everything else. Inside it: Sprint Planning (agree the Sprint Goal and what to build), the Daily Scrum or standup (a 15-minute daily re-plan by the Developers), the Sprint Review (show the working increment to stakeholders and gather feedback), and the Sprint Retrospective (the team reflects on improving its own process). The three artifacts are the Product Backlog (the ordered master wishlist), the Sprint Backlog (the slice committed to this Sprint plus the plan to build it), and the Increment (the actual done, usable output). Each artifact carries a commitment: the Product Goal, the Sprint Goal, and the Definition of Done respectively.
lessons.example: At a company like Cars24, a data team's Product Owner might rank "dealer inventory dashboard" above "internal cost report" because sales need it urgently. In Sprint Planning the team pulls it into the Sprint Backlog. A morning standup surfaces that the data pipeline broke overnight — a blocker the Scrum Master helps escalate. At the Sprint Review the analyst demos the working dashboard to the sales head; at the Retrospective the team agrees to add automated data-quality checks so pipelines stop failing silently.
lessons.code:
SCRUM AT A GLANCE
  Roles      : Product Owner | Scrum Master | Developers
  Events     : Sprint (container) > Sprint Planning >
               Daily Scrum > Sprint Review > Retrospective
  Artifacts  : Product Backlog | Sprint Backlog | Increment
  Commitments: Product Goal | Sprint Goal | Definition of Done

TIMEBOXES (maximum, for a one-month Sprint; scale down
for shorter Sprints):
  Sprint ............. 1 month or less (commonly 2 weeks)
  Sprint Planning .... up to 8 hours
  Daily Scrum ........ 15 minutes, every day
  Sprint Review ...... up to 4 hours
  Sprint Retrospective up to 3 hours
lessons.tryit: On paper, draw three columns headed Roles, Events, Artifacts. From memory fill in all 3 roles, all 5 events, and all 3 artifacts. Then next to each role write one sentence on what they are accountable for. Check yourself against the official Scrum Guide.

mistakes.m: Thinking the Scrum Master is the team's manager who assigns tasks.
mistakes.fix: The Scrum Master is a coach and facilitator (a servant-leader) who removes impediments. The Developers self-organise and decide who does what; the Product Owner sets priority.

mistakes.m: Believing "Developers" means only software coders, so analysts and testers don't count.
mistakes.fix: In Scrum, "Developers" is everyone building the increment — data analysts, QA, designers, writers. If you do the work, you are a Developer in Scrum terms.

mistakes.m: Treating the Daily Standup as a status report delivered to a manager.
mistakes.fix: The Daily Scrum is by the Developers, for the Developers, to re-plan the next 24 hours toward the Sprint Goal. Keep it to 15 minutes — it is not a status update to a boss.

quiz.q: Who is accountable for ordering the Product Backlog to maximise value?
opts: The Scrum Master
The Product Owner
The Developers
The project sponsor
quiz.why: The Product Owner is accountable for Product Backlog management and for maximising the value of the product. The Scrum Master coaches the process, the Developers build, and a sponsor is not a Scrum role.
quiz.approach: Match accountability to role: "what/why plus priority" is the Product Owner. Eliminate the Scrum Master (process coach) and Developers (build the work).

quiz.q: What is the timebox for the Daily Scrum (standup)?
opts: 15 minutes
1 hour
30 minutes
As long as the manager needs for status updates
quiz.why: The Daily Scrum is timeboxed to 15 minutes and held every day of the Sprint by the Developers. The last option also wrongly frames it as a manager's status meeting.
quiz.approach: Recall that the standup is the shortest, most frequent event — 15 minutes daily. Any longer duration or "status to manager" framing is wrong.

quiz.q: Which of these is a Scrum ARTIFACT (not an event or a role)?
opts: Sprint Retrospective
Sprint Backlog
Product Owner
Sprint Planning
quiz.why: The three artifacts are the Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, and Increment. The Sprint Retrospective and Sprint Planning are events, and the Product Owner is a role.
quiz.approach: Sort each option into role, event, or artifact. Artifacts are outputs or things (backlogs, increment); events are meetings; roles are people.

lessons.title: User Stories, INVEST, Story Points & Velocity
lessons.concept:
In Agile we do not hand engineers a 50-page spec. We slice work into user stories — short descriptions of a feature from the user's point of view. The classic template, which originated at Connextra and was popularised by Mike Cohn, is: "As a [type of user], I want [a goal], so that [a benefit]." For example: "As a used-car dealer, I want to filter inventory by city, so that I can quickly see stock near my showroom." The "so that" is gold — it forces everyone to remember WHY we are building this. A story is a placeholder for a conversation, not a contract.

A good story passes the INVEST checklist, coined by Bill Wake: Independent (can be built on its own), Negotiable (details are discussed, not dictated), Valuable (delivers value to a user or customer), Estimable (the team can size it), Small (fits comfortably inside one Sprint), and Testable (you can objectively verify it is done). That last letter connects to acceptance criteria — the specific, testable conditions a story must satisfy to be accepted. A popular format is Given-When-Then: "Given I am on the inventory page, When I select Gurugram, Then only Gurugram cars appear." Clear acceptance criteria kill ambiguity and prevent the dreaded "but that's not what I meant" at demo time.

Now estimation and forecasting. Teams size stories not in hours but in story points — a single relative measure that blends effort, complexity, and uncertainty. Many teams use a modified, Fibonacci-like scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 20, 40, 100) because bigger things are inherently more uncertain, so the gaps widen to reflect that. A common technique is Planning Poker, where everyone reveals an estimate at the same time to avoid anchoring on the loudest voice. Add up the points a team completes each Sprint and you get its velocity — the team's average delivery rate. If a team averages 30 points per Sprint and a feature's backlog is 120 points, you can forecast roughly four Sprints. Velocity is a planning tool for one team's own forecasting — never a productivity score to compare teams or to pressure people.
lessons.example: Compare two stories. "As a sales manager, I want a daily revenue KPI tile, so that I can track targets" might be a 3 — a well-understood, small query. "As an analyst, I want a churn-prediction model wired into the dashboard, so that managers see at-risk dealers" might be a 13 — new data, modelling uncertainty, and integration risk. Both might take "about a week" on the calendar, but the second carries far more complexity and uncertainty. That is exactly what story points capture and raw hours miss.
lessons.code:
STORY TEMPLATE
  As a [user], I want [goal], so that [benefit].

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (Given-When-Then)
  Given [context], When [action], Then [expected result].

VELOCITY — average of last 3 Sprints (Excel)
  Sprint points in B2:B4 = 28, 32, 30
  =AVERAGE(B2:B4)            -> 30 points per Sprint

FORECAST — Sprints to clear a backlog (Excel)
  Backlog points in E1 = 120 ,  Velocity in E2 = 30
  =ROUNDUP(E1/E2, 0)         -> 4 Sprints
lessons.tryit: Take one thing you want to build at work, say a "weekly sales dashboard." Write it as a proper user story (As a..., I want..., so that...). Add two acceptance criteria in Given-When-Then form. Finally give it a story-point estimate (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, or 13) and one sentence explaining why.

mistakes.m: Writing story points as if 1 point equals 1 hour or 1 day.
mistakes.fix: Points are relative (effort plus complexity plus uncertainty), not time. Two 3-point stories need not take equal hours. Keep points and hours separate, or velocity forecasting breaks down.

mistakes.m: Skipping the "so that" benefit, or skipping acceptance criteria.
mistakes.fix: Without the benefit you lose the WHY and risk building the wrong thing; without acceptance criteria, "done" is subjective. Always include both.

mistakes.m: Comparing velocity across teams — Team A does 40, Team B does 25, so B is lazy.
mistakes.fix: Points are calibrated separately by each team, so velocities are not comparable. Velocity is only for a team forecasting its OWN future Sprints.

links.t: Atlassian: User stories with examples and a template

quiz.q: What does the "I" in INVEST stand for?
opts: Integrated
Independent
Iterative
Important
quiz.why: INVEST stands for Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, and Testable, so the "I" is Independent — a story should stand on its own without depending on other stories. "Integrated" is nearly the opposite of independent, "Iterative" sounds Agile but is not in the mnemonic, and "Important" is a decoy for the "V" (Valuable).
quiz.approach: Spell out the six INVEST letters. Only "Independent" fits the "I"; watch for Agile-flavoured decoys like "Iterative" that are not part of the acronym.

quiz.q: A team's velocity over the last three Sprints was 28, 32, and 30 points. A new feature is estimated at 120 points. Roughly how many Sprints will it take?
opts: About 2 Sprints
About 4 Sprints
About 8 Sprints
About 40 Sprints
quiz.why: First average the recent velocities: (28 + 32 + 30) / 3 = 30 points per Sprint. Then divide the backlog: 120 / 30 = 4 Sprints. "About 40" is the classic trap — dividing 120 by the 3 past Sprints instead of by the velocity; "2" and "8" come from using the wrong divisor (about 60 or 15) rather than the velocity of 30.
quiz.approach: Two steps: (1) average the last three velocities to get 30, (2) divide the 120-point backlog by that 30. Always divide by the rate (velocity), never by the number of past Sprints.

quiz.q: Which is the best-written user story?
opts: Build a filter.
The system shall implement a multi-parameter query interface per spec section 4.2.
As a dealer, I want to filter inventory by city, so that I can see stock near my showroom.
Make the dashboard better for everyone somehow.
quiz.why: A good story names the user, the goal, and the benefit (As a... I want... so that...) and is testable. The first is too vague, the second is a spec rather than a story, and the last is unmeasurable.
quiz.approach: Look for the three parts — who, what, and why — plus testability. Only the dealer story has all three; reject vague or spec-style wording.

lessons.title: Burndown/Burnup, Kanban, WIP & the Analyst's Role
lessons.concept:
Once a team commits to a Sprint, how do you see whether it is on track? Two charts help. A burndown chart plots work remaining (Y axis, in story points) against time (X axis, days of the Sprint), sloping down toward zero; a straight "ideal" line shows the pace you would need, and the actual line reveals whether you are ahead or behind. A burnup chart instead plots work completed as a rising line, with a separate top line for total scope. The burnup's superpower is that if the customer adds work mid-Sprint, the scope line jumps up — so you can SEE scope creep, which a burndown hides (a flat burndown could mean "no progress" OR "progress cancelled out by newly added work"). For a data analyst these are simply two ways of visualising the same data — a skill you already have.

Not every team works in fixed Sprints. Kanban is an alternative Agile approach built for continuous flow — ideal for support, operations, and very often data and BI teams whose work arrives unpredictably ("can you pull these numbers by end of day?"). Kanban rests on a few core practices: visualise the work on a board (columns like To Do, In Progress, Review, Done), limit work-in-progress, and manage flow. A WIP limit caps how many items may sit in a column at once — say "In Progress: max 3." This feels backwards (surely doing more at once is faster?) but the opposite is true: capping WIP forces the team to finish before starting, exposes bottlenecks, and shortens the time each item takes. Stop starting, start finishing.

Two flow metrics matter. Cycle time is how long an item takes from when work actually starts on it to when it is done; lead time runs from when it is first requested to when it is done, so lead time is always at least as long as cycle time because of waiting in the queue. Little's Law ties flow together: Cycle Time = WIP / Throughput. So if 6 tasks are in progress and the team finishes 3 per day, each takes about 2 days — and lowering WIP directly lowers cycle time. This is where a data analyst thrives inside an agile team: you are both a Developer building increments (dashboards, models, and pipeline fixes taken as user stories from the backlog) AND the person who can measure the team's own flow. Plot cycle-time trends, spot the bottleneck column, and bring that evidence to the Retrospective. The analyst who says "our Review column is the bottleneck — items wait three days there" turns vague grumbling into an improvement the team can act on.
lessons.example: A Cars24 BI team runs a Kanban board with a WIP limit of 3 on "In Progress." A rush of ad-hoc requests piles up. The analyst measures cycle time and finds requests take 6 days on average, with 4 of those days stuck in "Review" because only one senior can approve. Bringing this to the standup, the team adds a second reviewer and pauses new work until the queue clears — and cycle time drops to 2.5 days. That is agile flow management driven by an analyst's own data.
lessons.code:
LITTLE'S LAW (stable system)
  Cycle Time = WIP / Throughput
  WIP = 6 items in progress
  Throughput = 3 items finished per day
  Cycle Time = 6 / 3 = 2 days per item

CYCLE TIME from dates (Excel)
  Start date in A2, Done date in B2
  =B2 - A2                 -> days for that item
  Average:  =AVERAGE(C2:C50)

READING A BURNDOWN
  Actual line ABOVE the ideal line = behind schedule
  Actual line BELOW the ideal line = ahead of schedule
lessons.tryit: List five tasks you finished recently with their start and finish dates. In a spreadsheet compute each task's cycle time (=FinishDate - StartDate) and the average. Then ask: where did items spend the most time waiting? That waiting stage is the column a WIP limit would most help.

mistakes.m: Assuming a flat burndown always means the team did nothing.
mistakes.fix: A flat burndown can also mean new scope was added that cancelled out completed work. Use a burnup chart, which shows scope and progress as separate lines, to tell the two apart.

mistakes.m: Thinking higher WIP limits (more tasks at once) make a team faster.
mistakes.fix: By Little's Law, more WIP means longer cycle time. Lower WIP finishes items sooner. The mantra is "stop starting, start finishing."

mistakes.m: Confusing cycle time with lead time.
mistakes.fix: Lead time is measured from the moment work is REQUESTED; cycle time starts only when work actually BEGINS. Lead time is at least as long as cycle time because of queue and waiting time.

quiz.q: What advantage does a burnup chart have over a burndown chart?
opts: It uses fewer data points
It shows scope changes as a separate line, revealing scope creep
It does not require story points
It only works for Kanban, not Scrum
quiz.why: A burnup plots completed work and total scope as two separate lines, so added scope shows up as the scope line rising — something a single burndown line hides. The other options are inaccurate.
quiz.approach: Remember that a burnup has two lines (done plus scope). Its benefit is visibility of scope change. Reject options about data volume or tool exclusivity.

quiz.q: A team has 8 items in progress and finishes 4 items per day. Using Little's Law, the average cycle time is:
opts: 32 days
2 days
4 days
0.5 days
quiz.why: Little's Law gives Cycle Time = WIP / Throughput = 8 / 4 = 2 days. "32" multiplies (8 x 4) instead of dividing; "4" just echoes the throughput number (4 per day) rather than computing the ratio; "0.5" inverts the formula (4 / 8).
quiz.approach: Apply Cycle Time = WIP / Throughput: divide the 8 items in progress by the 4 finished per day to get 2 days — divide, don't multiply or invert.

quiz.q: Why do Kanban teams set WIP (work-in-progress) limits?
opts: To let everyone start as many tasks as possible
To force finishing work before starting new work, reducing cycle time and exposing bottlenecks
To remove the need for a Done column
To guarantee a fixed two-week sprint length
quiz.why: WIP limits cap concurrent work so teams finish before starting, which lowers cycle time (Little's Law) and makes bottleneck columns visible. Kanban uses continuous flow, not fixed sprints, so the last option is wrong.
quiz.approach: Recall the mantra "stop starting, start finishing." The correct answer limits concurrent work to improve flow, rather than maximising started tasks.

lessons.title: Agile vs Waterfall & the Agile Manifesto
lessons.concept:
Waterfall runs a project as a fixed sequence of stages — requirements, then design, then build, then test, then release — where each stage is signed off before the next begins. It works when requirements are truly frozen (think a bridge or a regulatory filing), but for software it front-loads every decision to the moment you know the least. In an Indian IT services context, many teams grew up on Waterfall because SOW-based client contracts demanded a locked scope, a fixed timeline, and a big-bang UAT at the end. The pain is familiar: by the time the client sees a working screen after six months, the market has moved, and change requests become a billing war.

Agile inverts this. Instead of one long pipeline, you deliver in short cycles (typically 1-4 week iterations), showing working software to stakeholders repeatedly and adjusting course each time. The 2001 Agile Manifesto crystallised this with four value statements: individuals and interactions OVER processes and tools; working software OVER comprehensive documentation; customer collaboration OVER contract negotiation; and responding to change OVER following a plan. The word 'over' is the whole point — the items on the right still have value, but when you must trade off, you favour the left.

A crucial nuance often missed in Indian interviews: Agile is a mindset defined by the Manifesto's 4 values and 12 principles, while Scrum and Kanban are specific frameworks that implement that mindset. Saying 'we do Agile' is like saying 'we play sport' — Scrum is the actual game with rules. Also, Agile does not mean 'no documentation' or 'no planning'; it means just enough, produced just in time, and revised as you learn.
lessons.example: A Bengaluru fintech building a UPI-based lending app chose 2-week sprints over a 9-month Waterfall plan. In sprint 3 they demoed the loan-eligibility screen to a lending partner, who realised the CIBIL-score threshold logic was wrong. Because only 6 weeks of work existed, the fix cost days. Under Waterfall, that flaw would have surfaced in month-8 UAT and forced a costly redesign.
lessons.code:
The 4 values of the Agile Manifesto (each is 'X over Y'):
1. Individuals and interactions  OVER  processes and tools
2. Working software             OVER  comprehensive documentation
3. Customer collaboration       OVER  contract negotiation
4. Responding to change         OVER  following a plan

Mnemonic: I-W-C-R  ->  'I Will Cook Rice'
(Individuals, Working software, Customer collaboration, Responding to change)
lessons.tryit: Take one project you know (a college fest website, a dashboard at work). Write two columns: how it would run under Waterfall (list the sequential phases and the single delivery point) versus Agile (list 3-4 short iterations and what working slice each would deliver). Then map each of the 4 Manifesto values to one concrete decision you'd make differently.

mistakes.m: Believing Agile means 'no documentation' or 'no planning', so teams skip essential design docs and get chaos.
mistakes.fix: The Manifesto values working software OVER comprehensive documentation — 'over', not 'instead of'. Produce just-enough documentation, just in time, and keep planning continuously rather than once.

mistakes.m: Using 'Agile' and 'Scrum' as synonyms in interviews and standups.
mistakes.fix: Agile is the mindset (4 values, 12 principles from the Manifesto). Scrum and Kanban are frameworks that implement it. Name the framework you actually use.

mistakes.m: Assuming Waterfall is always wrong.
mistakes.fix: Waterfall suits genuinely stable, well-understood requirements (regulatory, hardware, fixed-scope contracts). Choose the approach based on how much requirements are expected to change.

quiz.q: The Agile Manifesto states 'Working software over comprehensive documentation.' What does 'over' signify here?
opts: Documentation should never be produced
When trading off, favour working software, but documentation still has value
Software and documentation are equally weighted always
Documentation must be completed before any software
quiz.why: 'Over' expresses a preference in trade-offs, not elimination. The Manifesto explicitly adds that there is still 'value in the items on the right' — so documentation genuinely matters; it is simply outranked when the two directly conflict. Reading 'over' as 'instead of' is the classic misinterpretation that pushes teams to abandon docs entirely.
quiz.approach: Focus on the exact wording 'over' rather than 'instead of'. The Manifesto explicitly says the right-hand items have value — eliminate any option implying total exclusion or strict equality.

quiz.q: Which statement correctly distinguishes Agile from Scrum?
opts: Agile is a framework; Scrum is a mindset
They are identical, interchangeable terms
Agile is a mindset (values and principles); Scrum is a framework that implements it
Agile and Scrum are competing frameworks you must pick between
quiz.why: Agile is the overarching mindset defined by the Manifesto's 4 values and 12 principles. Scrum is a concrete framework (accountabilities, events, artifacts) that puts that mindset into practice — so they are not rivals you choose between, nor synonyms, nor reversed in hierarchy.
quiz.approach: Recall the hierarchy: mindset (Agile) sits above frameworks (Scrum, Kanban). Reject any option that swaps these roles, equates them, or frames them as either/or alternatives.

quiz.q: For which situation is Waterfall most defensible over Agile?
opts: A startup exploring an uncertain new market
A project with genuinely stable, well-understood, fixed requirements
A product needing frequent user feedback
A team that expects requirements to change often
quiz.why: Waterfall's sequential, sign-off-driven flow suits stable, well-understood requirements. Agile is favoured precisely when requirements are uncertain or expected to change — as in the other three options, which all describe high-volatility contexts.
quiz.approach: Match the method to requirement volatility. High change or uncertainty points to Agile; genuine stability and known scope is where Waterfall's up-front planning pays off.

lessons.title: The Scrum Framework: Roles, Events & Artifacts
lessons.concept:
Scrum is a lightweight framework for delivering value in short, fixed-length cycles called Sprints (one month or less, most commonly 2 weeks in Indian product teams). The 2020 Scrum Guide defines exactly three accountabilities, five events, and three artifacts — memorise these boundaries, because interviewers love probing them. The three accountabilities are the Product Owner (owns the 'what' and the priority — maximises value, manages the Product Backlog), the Scrum Master (a coach and impediment-remover who ensures Scrum is understood and enacted — a servant-leader, NOT a project manager who assigns tasks), and the Developers (the people who build the Increment; note that in Scrum every builder is a 'Developer', including a data analyst or QA).

The five events all happen inside the Sprint, which is the container event. Sprint Planning (the team decides what to deliver this Sprint and how — output is the Sprint Goal and Sprint Backlog). The Daily Scrum (a 15-minute daily sync for the Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and re-plan the day — it is not a status report to the manager). The Sprint Review (at the Sprint's end, the team and stakeholders inspect the Increment and adapt the Product Backlog — this is about the product). The Sprint Retrospective (the team inspects how it worked — people, process, tools — and commits to improvements — this is about the process). Timeboxes matter: for a one-month Sprint, Planning is up to 8 hours, Review up to 4 hours, Retrospective up to 3 hours; scale down for shorter Sprints.

The three artifacts each carry a commitment. The Product Backlog is the single, ordered list of everything that might be needed — its commitment is the Product Goal. The Sprint Backlog is what the Developers pick for this Sprint plus a plan — its commitment is the Sprint Goal. The Increment is a concrete, usable step toward the Product Goal — its commitment is the Definition of Done, the shared quality checklist an item must meet to be called 'done'. A key rule: an Increment is only valuable once it meets the Definition of Done.
lessons.example: At a Gurugram e-commerce company, a squad runs 2-week Sprints. The Product Owner (a former category manager) orders the backlog so 'one-click reorder' sits on top. In Sprint Planning the team commits to the Sprint Goal 'let repeat buyers reorder in one tap'. Each morning at 10:00 the six Developers — including one data analyst — hold a 15-minute Daily Scrum. At Sprint end, the Review demos the working reorder button to stakeholders; the Retrospective surfaces that flaky test data slowed everyone, and the Scrum Master takes an action to fix the test environment.
lessons.code:
Scrum at a glance (2020 Scrum Guide):

3 ACCOUNTABILITIES
  - Product Owner  -> maximises value, owns Product Backlog (the 'what/why')
  - Scrum Master   -> coaches, removes impediments (servant-leader, NOT a boss)
  - Developers     -> build the Increment (the 'how')

5 EVENTS  (all inside the Sprint container)
  - The Sprint            -> fixed length, <= 1 month
  - Sprint Planning       -> defines Sprint Goal + Sprint Backlog
  - Daily Scrum           -> 15 min, Developers re-plan the day
  - Sprint Review         -> inspect the Increment (product)
  - Sprint Retrospective  -> inspect the process (people/tools)

3 ARTIFACTS  (each has a commitment)
  - Product Backlog  -> commitment: Product Goal
  - Sprint Backlog   -> commitment: Sprint Goal
  - Increment        -> commitment: Definition of Done
lessons.tryit: Draw a one-page map of a 2-week Sprint for a team you can imagine (say, an analytics team building a churn dashboard). Place all five events on a timeline, label who leads each, note the timebox, and beside each of the three artifacts write its commitment. Then write one sentence explaining why the Daily Scrum is for the Developers, not a status update to a manager.

mistakes.m: Treating the Scrum Master as a project manager who assigns tasks and chases deadlines.
mistakes.fix: The Scrum Master is a servant-leader and coach who removes impediments and safeguards the process. The Developers self-manage and pull their own work; the Product Owner sets priority.

mistakes.m: Confusing the Sprint Review with the Retrospective.
mistakes.fix: The Review inspects the PRODUCT (the Increment) with stakeholders. The Retrospective inspects the PROCESS (how the team worked) and is for the Scrum Team. Remember: Review = product, Retro = people/process.

mistakes.m: Calling something 'done' when it only meets one person's idea of finished.
mistakes.fix: 'Done' means it meets the shared Definition of Done — the team's agreed quality bar. Without meeting it, the work is not part of a releasable Increment.

mistakes.m: Believing the Daily Scrum must follow the fixed 'three questions' (what I did yesterday / will do today / any blockers).
mistakes.fix: The 2020 Scrum Guide dropped the mandatory three-question format. The Developers may run the Daily Scrum however they like, as long as it advances progress toward the Sprint Goal and produces an actionable plan for the day.

quiz.q: According to the 2020 Scrum Guide, what is the commitment associated with the Increment artifact?
opts: The Sprint Goal
The Product Goal
The Definition of Done
The Sprint Backlog
quiz.why: Each artifact has a commitment: Product Backlog -> Product Goal, Sprint Backlog -> Sprint Goal, and Increment -> Definition of Done. The Sprint Backlog is itself an artifact, not a commitment, so it is a trap. An Increment is only valuable once it meets the Definition of Done.
quiz.approach: Pair each artifact with its commitment. The Increment is about quality/usability, so its commitment is the Definition of Done — not a goal statement and not another artifact.

quiz.q: Which best describes the primary purpose of the Daily Scrum?
opts: A status report from Developers to the Scrum Master
A 15-minute event for Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and re-plan the day
A meeting where the Product Owner assigns new tasks
A review of the Increment with external stakeholders
quiz.why: The Daily Scrum is a 15-minute event for the Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the plan for the next day. It is not a status report, not a task-assignment meeting for the PO, and not the stakeholder-facing Sprint Review.
quiz.approach: Identify the audience and intent: Daily Scrum is by and for the Developers to self-manage. Eliminate options that make it a report to a manager, an assignment forum, or a stakeholder demo.

quiz.q: A team demonstrates working software to stakeholders and adapts the Product Backlog based on their feedback. Which event is this?
opts: Sprint Retrospective
Sprint Planning
Sprint Review
Daily Scrum
quiz.why: Inspecting the Increment with stakeholders and adapting the Product Backlog is the Sprint Review (product focus). The Retrospective, by contrast, inspects the team's process; Planning sets up the Sprint; the Daily Scrum is a Developer-only re-plan.
quiz.approach: Key on 'stakeholders + Increment + adapt backlog' = Review (product). Contrast with the Retrospective, which is internal and process-focused.

lessons.title: User Stories, Acceptance Criteria (INVEST) & Sizing with Story Points
lessons.concept:
A user story is a short, plain-language description of a feature told from the user's perspective, usually in the template: 'As a [type of user], I want [some goal] so that [some benefit].' The 'so that' clause is not decoration — it forces you to state the value, which lets the Product Owner prioritise honestly. Stories are deliberately not full specifications; they are a promise for a conversation. Good stories follow the INVEST checklist (Bill Wake): Independent (can be built in any order), Negotiable (details are discussed, not dictated), Valuable (delivers value to a user or customer), Estimable (the team can size it), Small (fits comfortably in a Sprint), and Testable (you can verify it's done).

Acceptance criteria define the specific, testable conditions that must be true for the story to be accepted — they turn a vague wish into something verifiable. A popular format is Gherkin's Given/When/Then: Given some starting context, When an action happens, Then expect a result. Acceptance criteria differ from the Definition of Done: acceptance criteria are story-specific (does THIS feature behave correctly?), while the Definition of Done is a team-wide quality bar that applies to EVERY story (code reviewed, tested, documented). A story is only truly finished when it passes its acceptance criteria AND meets the Definition of Done.

Story points estimate the relative effort/complexity/uncertainty of a story, NOT the hours it takes. Teams size relatively — 'this story is about twice as hard as that one' — which is faster and more honest than guessing clock time. Many teams use a modified Fibonacci scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 20, 40, 100) because the widening gaps reflect growing uncertainty in bigger items. Points are a team-relative currency: a 5 on one team is not comparable to a 5 on another, and you must never use points to compare individuals' productivity. Estimation is often done collaboratively via Planning Poker, where each Developer reveals a card simultaneously and differences trigger a discussion.
lessons.example: An analytics team at a Pune SaaS firm writes: 'As a regional sales head, I want to filter the revenue dashboard by state so that I can spot underperforming territories.' Acceptance criteria (Given/When/Then): Given I am on the dashboard, When I select 'Maharashtra' from the state filter, Then all charts refresh to show only Maharashtra data within 2 seconds. In Planning Poker the team plays 3, 3, 5, 8 — the person who played 8 explains the state master data is messy, the team discusses, and settles on 5 points.
lessons.code:
USER STORY TEMPLATE
  As a <type of user>, I want <goal> so that <benefit>.

INVEST checklist for a good story:
  I - Independent   (orderable on its own)
  N - Negotiable    (a conversation, not a contract)
  V - Valuable      (delivers user/customer value)
  E - Estimable     (team can size it)
  S - Small         (fits within a Sprint)
  T - Testable      (has clear acceptance criteria)

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (Gherkin):
  Given <initial context>
  When  <action occurs>
  Then  <expected outcome>

STORY POINTS (modified Fibonacci):
  1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 20, 40, 100
  -> measure relative effort/complexity/uncertainty, NOT hours
lessons.tryit: Write one user story in the 'As a / I want / so that' format for a feature you'd want in an app you use daily. Add 3 acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then form. Then check your story against all six INVEST letters and note which letter is weakest and why. Finally, assign a story-point value from the Fibonacci scale and justify it relative to a simpler '2-point' baseline story you invent.

mistakes.m: Treating story points as disguised hours (e.g., '1 point = 1 day').
mistakes.fix: Points measure relative size — effort, complexity, and uncertainty combined. Estimate by comparison to reference stories, not by converting to a clock. Velocity later tells you how many points typically fit in a Sprint.

mistakes.m: Confusing acceptance criteria with the Definition of Done.
mistakes.fix: Acceptance criteria are unique to each story (does this feature work as intended?). The Definition of Done is a single team-wide quality bar applied to every story. A story needs both to be complete.

mistakes.m: Writing stories with no 'so that' / no user value, e.g. 'Add an index to the orders table.'
mistakes.fix: Frame from a user's benefit or rewrite technical tasks as enablers tied to value. If there is genuinely no user-facing value, it may be a task or a spike, not a user story.

links.t: Atlassian: User stories with examples & template

quiz.q: What do story points primarily measure?
opts: The exact number of hours a task will take
The relative effort, complexity, and uncertainty of an item
The number of Developers assigned to a story
The monetary cost of building a feature
quiz.why: Story points are a relative measure of effort, complexity, and uncertainty combined — deliberately not clock hours, because the same task takes different people different times and raw hours ignore complexity and risk. Teams size stories by comparison to a known reference story, then use velocity to convert points into a delivery forecast.
quiz.approach: Reject any answer that maps points to a fixed time, a headcount, or a rupee cost. Points are relative and team-specific; that is the defining property.

quiz.q: In the INVEST checklist, what does the 'N' stand for?
opts: Necessary
Negotiable
Numbered
Non-functional
quiz.why: INVEST = Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable. 'Negotiable' means a story's details are a conversation, not a rigid, pre-fixed contract — the other N-words are plausible-sounding traps that are not part of the mnemonic.
quiz.approach: Run through the mnemonic letter by letter. 'N' is Negotiable — capturing that stories are placeholders for discussion, not frozen specs.

quiz.q: How do acceptance criteria differ from the Definition of Done?
opts: They are the same thing
Acceptance criteria are story-specific; the Definition of Done is a team-wide bar for every story
Acceptance criteria apply to all stories; the Definition of Done is per-story
Only the Definition of Done is testable
quiz.why: Acceptance criteria are unique conditions for a specific story, while the Definition of Done is a shared quality standard applied to every story. Option 3 reverses the two; a story must satisfy both to be complete.
quiz.approach: Separate scope: 'this story' (acceptance criteria) versus 'every story' (Definition of Done). The one that is universal across all work is the Definition of Done.

lessons.title: Velocity, Burndown/Burnup, Kanban & the Data Analyst in an Agile Team
lessons.concept:
Once a team sizes work in story points, velocity is simply the number of points it actually completes per Sprint — and averaging the last 3-5 Sprints gives a realistic capacity for forecasting. If a team averages 24 points per Sprint and the backlog for a release totals 120 points, a rough forecast is about 5 Sprints. Velocity is a planning tool for the team's own use, never a cross-team performance league table — because points are team-relative, comparing velocities across teams (or pressuring a team to 'increase velocity') corrupts the estimates. A burndown chart plots work remaining (points or tasks) on the y-axis against time on the x-axis, sloping down toward zero — it answers 'are we on track to finish?'. A burnup chart instead plots work completed rising toward a separate scope line, which makes scope changes visible: if the scope line jumps up mid-Sprint, you can see that scope was added rather than the team falling behind.

Kanban is an alternative Agile approach focused on continuous flow rather than fixed-length Sprints. Its core practices are visualising work on a board (columns like To Do / In Progress / Review / Done) and limiting Work In Progress (WIP limits) — a hard cap on how many items may sit in a column at once. WIP limits are counter-intuitively powerful: by stopping people from starting new work, they force the team to finish current work, exposing bottlenecks (Little's Law underlies this). Two key Kanban metrics are cycle time (how long one item takes from 'work started' to 'done') and throughput (items completed per unit time). Lower, more predictable cycle time means faster, more reliable delivery. Kanban has no prescribed roles or Sprints, which is why support, ops, and analytics teams with unpredictable inflow often prefer it over Scrum.

So how does a data analyst actually fit into an Agile team? An analyst is a 'Developer' in Scrum terms — a builder of the Increment. Analyst work still becomes stories ('As a growth manager, I want a cohort-retention chart so that I can measure the effect of the June campaign'), gets acceptance criteria (correct SQL logic, matches source-of-truth numbers, loads under 3 seconds), and gets sized in points. Analysts contribute in every event: in Planning they flag data-availability risks; in the Daily Scrum they raise blockers like 'the events pipeline is delayed'; in the Review they demo the dashboard; in the Retrospective they suggest process fixes. Because analytics requests often arrive unpredictably (an urgent CXO question mid-Sprint), many analytics teams run Kanban or a Scrumban hybrid, reserving some WIP capacity for ad-hoc asks so a fixed Sprint commitment isn't blown up by every fire drill.
lessons.example: A data analyst at a Bengaluru consumer-internet firm sits in a 6-person squad. Their board has a WIP limit of 2 in 'In Progress'. A story — 'churn-driver breakdown by city' — is sized at 5 points. Mid-Sprint the VP asks for an urgent GMV number; because the team reserved one 'expedite' lane in their Scrumban board, the analyst handles it without breaking the Sprint Goal. Over the last four Sprints the squad's velocity was 22, 26, 24, 24, so for release planning they forecast ~24 points per Sprint. Their average cycle time for a dashboard story is 3.5 days.
lessons.code:
VELOCITY & FORECAST
  Velocity (this Sprint) = sum of story points of items that met Definition of Done
  Average velocity       = mean of last 3-5 Sprints
  Sprints to finish      = Total backlog points / Average velocity
  Example: 120 points / 24 avg = 5 Sprints

Excel (average of last 4 Sprints in B2:B5):
  =AVERAGE(B2:B5)
Excel (forecast Sprints, backlog in E1):
  =CEILING(E1 / AVERAGE(B2:B5), 1)

BURNDOWN vs BURNUP
  Burndown: y = work REMAINING, trends down to 0
  Burnup:   y = work COMPLETED rising toward a separate SCOPE line
            (burnup makes scope changes visible)

KANBAN FLOW METRICS
  Cycle time  = time from 'work started' to 'work done' (per item)
  Throughput  = items completed per unit time
  WIP limit   = max items allowed in a column at once (a hard cap)
lessons.tryit: Given a team's last five Sprint velocities — 18, 22, 20, 24, 21 — compute the average velocity and, for a 100-point release backlog, forecast how many Sprints are needed (round up). Then design a simple Kanban board (4 columns) for a data-analytics team, set a WIP limit for 'In Progress', and write one sentence explaining what the WIP limit forces the team to do when it's hit.

mistakes.m: Using velocity to compare or rank different teams, or setting 'increase velocity' as a target.
mistakes.fix: Story points are team-relative, so cross-team velocity comparison is meaningless. Making velocity a target invites point inflation, which corrupts forecasting. Use velocity only as one team's internal planning aid.

mistakes.m: Confusing burndown and burnup, or thinking neither shows scope creep.
mistakes.fix: Burndown shows work remaining trending to zero; burnup shows work completed against a separate scope line. Burnup makes added scope visible as a rising scope line — burndown can hide it.

mistakes.m: Setting WIP limits too high (or not at all), so everything is 'in progress' and nothing finishes.
mistakes.fix: Set a low, deliberate WIP limit. Its purpose is to stop starting and start finishing — when the limit is hit, the team swarms to clear the bottleneck before pulling new work.

quiz.q: A Scrum team's velocities for its last four Sprints were 20, 25, 22, and 25 points. The remaining release backlog is 138 points. Roughly how many more Sprints are needed?
opts: About 3
About 6
About 10
About 2
quiz.why: Average velocity = (20+25+22+25)/4 = 92/4 = 23. Sprints needed = 138 / 23 = 6. Forecasting divides the remaining backlog by the average velocity across recent Sprints, then rounds up.
quiz.approach: Average the recent velocities first, then divide the remaining backlog by that average and round up. 92/4 = 23; 138/23 = 6. Beware the trap of dividing by the sum (92) instead of the average, which wrongly gives ~2.

quiz.q: What is the main effect of setting a Work In Progress (WIP) limit in Kanban?
opts: It increases the number of tasks started simultaneously
It forces the team to finish current work before starting new work, exposing bottlenecks
It assigns story points to each item automatically
It replaces the need for a Definition of Done
quiz.why: A WIP limit caps how many items can sit in a column at once. When the cap is hit, no new work can start, so the team must finish or unblock in-progress items first — which surfaces bottlenecks and, by Little's Law (cycle time = WIP / throughput), shortens cycle time. The mantra is 'stop starting, start finishing.'
quiz.approach: Recall 'stop starting, start finishing'. The correct option describes limiting concurrent work to force completion, not increasing it — and a WIP limit neither estimates nor replaces the Definition of Done.

quiz.q: In Scrum terms, how is a data analyst on a cross-functional product squad best classified?
opts: As the Scrum Master, since they manage data
As a Developer — a builder of the Increment whose work is captured as stories
As the Product Owner, since they own the data
As an external stakeholder who only attends the Sprint Review
quiz.why: Scrum calls everyone who builds the Increment a 'Developer', including data analysts. Their work is written as user stories, given acceptance criteria, sized in points, and demoed in the Review — so they are neither the SM, the PO, nor a passive stakeholder.
quiz.approach: Map the role to the 'builds the Increment' definition. An analyst produces deliverables (dashboards, analyses), so they are a Developer, not the SM, PO, or a passive stakeholder.

lessons.title: Waterfall, Agile & the Manifesto: Choosing the Right Battle
lessons.concept:
Waterfall (often traced to Winston Royce's 1970 paper — which ironically presented the pure sequential model as risky and itself argued for iteration) is a phase-gated approach: requirements → design → build → test → deploy, each fully signed off before the next begins. It shines when requirements are stable and well understood and the cost of late change is severe — a bridge, certified hardware, or a fixed-scope government tender with a locked RFP. Its weakness in software is the cost-of-change curve: a defect caught during UAT costs far more than one caught mid-sprint, and requirements are rarely truly frozen.

Agile inverts the bet. Rather than one big-bang delivery, you ship small working increments frequently, inspect real feedback, and adapt. The 2001 Agile Manifesto (17 practitioners, Snowbird, Utah) captured this in four values — individuals and interactions over processes and tools; working software over comprehensive documentation; customer collaboration over contract negotiation; responding to change over following a plan — with the vital caveat that the items on the right still have value; the left is valued more. Twelve principles operationalise it: deliver early and continuously, welcome changing requirements even late, working software as the primary measure of progress, and a sustainable pace.

For an ambitious Indian knowledge worker, the advanced insight is that Agile-vs-Waterfall is not a religious war — it is a risk-management choice. A CARS24 pricing dashboard or a Swiggy feature where user behaviour is uncertain screams Agile. A regulated core-banking migration for an RBI-audited PSU bank, or a fixed-scope e-governance tender with penalty clauses, may still run largely waterfall — or a hybrid ('water-scrum-fall') where discovery is agile but delivery carries compliance gates. Mature practitioners diagnose the domain (is the problem merely complicated, or genuinely complex?) instead of defaulting to a label.
lessons.example: A Bengaluru IT-services firm bids for two projects in one quarter. Project A: a fixed-price ONDC integration with a signed 200-page spec and penalty clauses — run largely waterfall with agile sub-teams inside phases. Project B: a consumer-app MVP for a funded startup whose founder reshuffles the roadmap monthly — run Scrum with two-week sprints. Same firm, opposite methods, because the risk profiles differ.
lessons.code:
A simple 'method fit' scoring heuristic (score each 1-5):
- Requirements stability (5 = frozen & signed)
- Cost of late change (5 = catastrophic/regulated)
- Value of frequent feedback (5 = high uncertainty)

Excel decision rule:
=IF(AND(Stability<=2, Feedback>=4), "Agile",
   IF(AND(Stability>=4, CostLateChange>=4), "Waterfall / Hybrid", "Hybrid"))

Example: Stability=2, Feedback=5, CostLateChange=2 -> "Agile".
Example: Stability=5, Feedback=2, CostLateChange=5 -> "Waterfall / Hybrid".
lessons.tryit: Take one real project you know (e.g., your team's current dashboard request). Score it 1-5 on requirements stability, cost of late change, need for frequent feedback, and regulatory constraint. Decide Agile / Waterfall / Hybrid, then write two sentences justifying the call against the four Manifesto values.

mistakes.m: Treating Agile as 'no documentation' or 'no planning'.
mistakes.fix: The Manifesto values working software OVER comprehensive documentation — 'over', not 'instead of'. Agile teams still document and plan; they just do it just-in-time and keep it lean.

mistakes.m: Assuming Waterfall is always obsolete or wrong.
mistakes.fix: For stable, high-compliance, or safety-critical scope, phase-gated delivery can be the lower-risk choice. Match the method to the problem's uncertainty, not to fashion.

mistakes.m: Reciting the four values but ignoring the 12 principles.
mistakes.fix: The principles are where Agile becomes actionable — sustainable pace, continuous delivery, welcoming late change. Mature teams and interviewers test the principles, not just the slogan.

links.t: Twelve Principles behind the Agile Manifesto (agilemanifesto.org)

quiz.q: The Agile Manifesto states 'working software over comprehensive documentation.' What does the word 'over' most precisely mean?
opts: Documentation is banned in Agile projects
Both items have value, but the left is valued more
Documentation must be fully completed before any coding
Working software is the only deliverable that has any value
quiz.why: The Manifesto explicitly says that while there is value in the items on the right, the items on the left are valued more — it is a matter of priority, not prohibition.
quiz.approach: Read 'over' as 'prioritised above', then eliminate any option that says 'banned', 'only', or 'must be fully completed first'.

quiz.q: A PSU bank must migrate its core system under RBI audit with a fixed, signed 300-page spec and heavy penalties for defects. Which primary approach is most defensible?
opts: Pure Scrum with 1-week sprints and no documentation
A phased/waterfall or hybrid approach with compliance gates
Kanban with no WIP limits and no cadence
Abandon the project because it is not 'agile enough'
quiz.why: Frozen, signed scope plus regulatory penalties means the cost of late change is high and requirements are stable — the classic case where phase-gated or hybrid delivery lowers risk.
quiz.approach: Diagnose the risk profile first (stable + regulated), then pick the method that reduces the cost of late change rather than the trendiest label.

quiz.q: How many core values and principles does the Agile Manifesto define?
opts: 4 values and 12 principles
3 values and 5 principles
5 values and 3 pillars
4 pillars and 17 principles
quiz.why: The Manifesto has 4 values and 12 principles. The number 17 was the count of signatories, 5 is the count of Scrum values, and 3 is the number of empiricism pillars — common distractors.
quiz.approach: Anchor to '4 and 12' for the Manifesto, and keep it separate from Scrum's 5 values, 3 pillars, and 17 signatories.

lessons.title: The Scrum Machine: Accountabilities, Events & Commitments
lessons.concept:
Scrum (defined in the 2020 Scrum Guide by Ken Schwaber and Jeff Sutherland) is a lightweight framework founded on empiricism — knowledge comes from experience, and decisions from what is observed. Its three pillars are transparency, inspection, and adaptation, upheld by five values: commitment, focus, openness, respect, and courage. A Scrum Team is typically ten or fewer people, cross-functional and self-managing, with no sub-teams or hierarchies. It holds three accountabilities (the 2020 Guide deliberately avoids the word 'roles'): the Product Owner, the Scrum Master, and the Developers.

The Product Owner is a single accountable person (never a committee) who maximises product value and owns the Product Backlog — its content, ordering, and transparency. The Developers are those who build a usable Increment each Sprint; they own the Sprint Backlog, instil quality through the Definition of Done, and adapt their plan daily. The Scrum Master is a true leader who serves the team and organisation, accountable for the Scrum Team's effectiveness — coaching self-management, facilitating events, and removing impediments. Crucially, the Scrum Master is not a task-assigning project manager, and the Product Owner is not the Developers' boss.

The Sprint is the heartbeat — a fixed cadence of one month or less that contains every other event: Sprint Planning (the what, why, and how; timeboxed to 8 hours for a one-month Sprint), the Daily Scrum (15 minutes for the Developers to inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal), the Sprint Review (inspect the Increment with stakeholders, up to 4 hours), and the Sprint Retrospective (inspect people, process, and tools, up to 3 hours). Three artifacts each carry a commitment: the Product Backlog commits to the Product Goal, the Sprint Backlog commits to the Sprint Goal, and the Increment commits to the Definition of Done. At a Gurugram product firm running two-week sprints, halve those timeboxes — the maxima scale with Sprint length.
lessons.example: A CARS24 team's fortnightly sprint: Monday 10am Sprint Planning sets the Sprint Goal 'Sellers can upload RC documents from mobile.' The Daily Scrum at 10am is a 15-minute stand-up focused only on progress toward that goal and impediments (a blocked API, a pending legal sign-off) — not a status report to the manager. On the second Friday, 3pm Sprint Review demos the working upload flow to the business head; 4pm Retrospective surfaces that vague acceptance criteria caused rework, so the team commits to a 'three-amigos' refinement next sprint. Unfinished work is not silently carried over — it returns to the Product Backlog for re-ordering.
lessons.code:
Timeboxes scale with Sprint length. Maxima for a ONE-MONTH Sprint, and the typical value for a TWO-WEEK Sprint:

Sprint Planning:     <= 8h  ->  ~4h
Daily Scrum:         15 min ->  15 min (fixed, every day)
Sprint Review:       <= 4h  ->  ~2h
Sprint Retrospective:<= 3h  ->  ~1.5h

Artifact -> Commitment map (memorise this):
Product Backlog  -> Product Goal
Sprint Backlog   -> Sprint Goal
Increment        -> Definition of Done
lessons.tryit: Map a 6-person analytics squad to the three Scrum accountabilities — name who is Product Owner, Scrum Master, and Developers. Then, for a two-week sprint, put concrete calendar slots and durations on all five events. Finally, flag one anti-pattern you currently see (e.g., a manager running the Daily Scrum as a status check-in to himself).

mistakes.m: Running the Daily Scrum as a status meeting reported to the Scrum Master or manager.
mistakes.fix: It is a 15-minute inspection by the Developers, for the Developers, focused on progress toward the Sprint Goal and on impediments — not a boss check-in.

mistakes.m: Treating the Product Owner as a committee or a passive ticket-forwarder.
mistakes.fix: The 2020 Guide makes the PO a single accountable person who orders the backlog and can say 'no'. Shared PO accountability dilutes decision-making and slows the team.

mistakes.m: Confusing the three empiricism pillars with the five Scrum values.
mistakes.fix: Pillars = transparency, inspection, adaptation (the process-control theory). Values = commitment, focus, openness, respect, courage (team behaviour). Keep them distinct.

quiz.q: In the 2020 Scrum Guide, which artifact is bound to the Definition of Done as its commitment?
opts: Product Backlog
Sprint Backlog
Increment
Sprint burndown chart
quiz.why: Each artifact has a commitment: Product Backlog -> Product Goal, Sprint Backlog -> Sprint Goal, and the Increment -> Definition of Done. A burndown chart is not even a Scrum artifact.
quiz.approach: Recall the three artifact-to-commitment pairs, then match 'Definition of Done' to the Increment.

quiz.q: For a one-month Sprint, what is the maximum timebox for Sprint Planning, and how does it scale for a two-week Sprint?
opts: 8 hours; typically about 4 hours for a two-week sprint
4 hours; fixed regardless of sprint length
15 minutes; the same for every sprint
24 hours; it scales up for shorter sprints
quiz.why: Sprint Planning is timeboxed to a maximum of 8 hours for a one-month Sprint and is usually shorter for shorter Sprints — roughly half for a two-week Sprint.
quiz.approach: Anchor to the one-month maxima, then remember event timeboxes shrink proportionally as the Sprint gets shorter.

quiz.q: Who is accountable for managing and ordering the Product Backlog?
opts: The Scrum Master
The Developers collectively
The Product Owner
The stakeholders during Sprint Review
quiz.why: The Product Owner is the single person accountable for maximising value and for Product Backlog management, including its ordering and transparency.
quiz.approach: Tie 'Product Backlog ownership and value maximisation' directly to the Product Owner accountability.

lessons.title: User Stories, INVEST, Story Points & Velocity
lessons.concept:
A user story is a short, user-centred description of a need, classically in the Connextra format: 'As a <role>, I want <goal>, so that <benefit>.' It is not a mini-spec but a placeholder for a conversation — remember the 3 Cs: Card, Conversation, Confirmation. Strong stories pass Bill Wake's INVEST test: Independent (minimal dependencies), Negotiable (details stay flexible), Valuable (delivers value to a user or customer), Estimable (the team can size it), Small (fits inside a sprint), and Testable (has clear acceptance criteria). Acceptance criteria make 'done' objective — often written in Given/When/Then (Gherkin) form.

Advanced teams estimate in story points — a relative measure blending effort, complexity, and uncertainty, not hours. They commonly use a modified Fibonacci scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 20, 40, 100) because larger items carry proportionally more uncertainty, and use Planning Poker to surface disagreement before it becomes rework. Velocity is the sum of points completed (meeting the Definition of Done) per sprint. Averaged over the last few sprints it forecasts capacity: a team averaging 30 points against a 180-point release backlog should expect roughly six sprints — a range, not a promise. Velocity is team-specific and must never be used to compare or rank teams, nor as an individual performance metric, or it gets gamed through point inflation.

Picture a fintech squad in Pune building UPI autopay. The story 'As a user, I want to set a monthly SIP autopay so that I never miss an investment' is valuable and testable but too big — an epic. Split it into mandate creation, mandate approval, and failure-retry stories, each INVEST-compliant, pointed 5, 3, and 8. Over four sprints the team completes 28, 32, 30, and 34 points — an average near 31. When the business asks 'can we launch by Diwali?', the analyst forecasts using a velocity range rather than a single false date, and explicitly notes that points measure this team's relative sizing, not man-days.
lessons.example: Splitting an epic 'Seller onboarding on a marketplace'. Weak story: 'Build onboarding' (not small, not testable). INVEST stories: (1) 'As a new seller, I want to register with my GSTIN so that I can list products.' AC: Given a valid 15-character GSTIN, When I submit, Then my account is created and I receive a verification email. (2) 'As a seller, I want to upload a cancelled cheque so that payouts can be set up.' Each is independently demoable and independently testable.
lessons.code:
Modified Fibonacci scale: 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 20, 40, 100

Velocity (average of last N sprints), Excel:
=AVERAGE(B2:B5)   e.g. AVERAGE(28,32,30,34) = 31

Forecast (sprints) = remaining points / average velocity:
=ROUNDUP(180/31, 0)  ->  6 sprints

Give a RANGE, not a single number:
Optimistic:  =ROUNDUP(180/34,0)  -> 6 sprints (fastest observed velocity)
Pessimistic: =ROUNDUP(180/28,0)  -> 7 sprints (slowest observed velocity)
lessons.tryit: Take a real request from your work (e.g., 'Build a churn dashboard'). Rewrite it as three INVEST user stories, each with a Given/When/Then acceptance criterion, assign each a Fibonacci story point (1-13), and — assuming a team velocity of 20 points per sprint — estimate how many sprints the set will take.

mistakes.m: Converting story points to hours (e.g., '1 point = 8 hours').
mistakes.fix: Points are relative and blend complexity and uncertainty; fixing an hours conversion reintroduces false precision and defeats their purpose. Forecast with velocity instead.

mistakes.m: Comparing velocity across teams or using it as an individual KPI.
mistakes.fix: Velocity is a team-local forecasting tool. Cross-team comparison and individual targets cause point inflation and gaming — measure delivered outcomes and value, not points.

mistakes.m: Writing stories with no acceptance criteria, so 'done' is subjective.
mistakes.fix: Add testable Given/When/Then criteria (the 'T' in INVEST). Without them, Sprint Review becomes an argument about whether the work is actually finished.

quiz.q: In INVEST, what does the 'E' stand for, and why does it matter?
opts: Efficient — the story must minimise cost
Estimable — the team can size it, and a story that can't be sized flags a need for more refinement
Executable — the code must compile and run
Essential — only must-have features qualify
quiz.why: The 'E' is Estimable. If a story cannot be sized, that is a signal it needs splitting, more detail, or a spike before it enters a sprint.
quiz.approach: Recall the six INVEST words, then match 'E' to 'Estimable' and reason about what an un-estimable story is telling you.

quiz.q: A manager wants to compare Team A's velocity (45) with Team B's (25) to decide who is 'more productive.' Why is this flawed?
opts: Velocity is a team-relative unit; points aren't calibrated across teams, so the numbers aren't comparable
Team A is clearly almost twice as productive as Team B
Velocity should first be converted to hours to compare fairly
Both teams should simply merge their backlogs
quiz.why: Story points are calibrated within a single team, so one team's '5' is not another team's '5'. Cross-team velocity comparison is meaningless and invites gaming.
quiz.approach: Ask whether the unit is standardised across teams; since points are team-local, reject any option that treats the numbers as directly comparable.

quiz.q: A team's last four sprint velocities are 28, 32, 30, 34. About how many sprints to clear a 150-point backlog using the average?
opts: About 3
About 5
About 8
About 12
quiz.why: The average velocity is about 31, and 150 / 31 is roughly 4.8, which rounds up to about 5 sprints.
quiz.approach: Average the velocities, divide the backlog by the average, then round up to whole sprints.

lessons.title: Kanban, Flow Metrics & the Data Analyst on an Agile Team
lessons.concept:
Kanban is a flow-based method (rooted in Toyota's lean production and adapted for knowledge work by David Anderson) built on six core practices: visualising work, limiting work-in-progress (WIP), managing flow, making policies explicit, implementing feedback loops, and improving collaboratively. Unlike Scrum, it has no fixed sprints, no prescribed roles, and no timeboxed commitment — you pull the next item when capacity frees up. Its signature lever is the WIP limit: a hard cap on items allowed in a column (for example, 'In Progress <= 3'). Limiting WIP forces the team to finish before starting, exposes bottlenecks, and — counter-intuitively — raises throughput by cutting context-switching and queueing.

Kanban's core metrics are cycle time (elapsed time from when work starts to when it is done), lead time (from the customer's request to delivery), and throughput (items completed per unit time). They are linked by Little's Law: average WIP = average throughput x average cycle time, so cycle time = WIP / throughput. If a BI team holds 12 tickets in progress and finishes 3 per day, average cycle time is about 4 days — and the fastest way to shorten it is to lower WIP, not to work longer hours. Cumulative flow diagrams and cycle-time scatterplots let a team forecast with percentiles ('85% of tickets finish within 6 days') instead of guesses.

A data analyst rarely owns shippable app code, yet lives inside the same cadence. In a Scrum squad at a Gurugram e-commerce firm, the analyst's work becomes backlog items too — 'As a category manager, I want a returns-rate dashboard so that I can flag high-return SKUs' — with acceptance criteria and story points. They join refinement (sizing analysis effort, flagging data-availability risk), Sprint Planning, the Daily Scrum (reporting blockers such as a broken data pipeline), and the Sprint Review (demoing a validated dashboard as the Increment). Because analytics is interrupt-driven — 'just pull this one number' arrives daily — many analytics and BI teams run Kanban or Scrumban: a visible board with WIP limits and swimlanes for 'ad-hoc' versus 'planned', tracking cycle time to negotiate realistic SLAs instead of dropping everything for the loudest requester. Spikes (timeboxed research stories) handle exploratory questions whose scope is unknown up front.
lessons.example: A four-person analytics team at a logistics startup switches from firefighting to Kanban. Columns: Backlog -> Analysis -> Review -> Done, with WIP limits (Analysis <= 4, Review <= 2) and a separate 'urgent' swimlane capped at 1. Before: 15 tickets 'in progress', nothing shipping, cycle time about 11 days. After enforcing WIP limits, in-progress drops to 6 and cycle time falls to about 4 days — the same people, just less multitasking. They now tell business partners 'standard requests: 85% done within 5 working days', backed by their scatterplot.
lessons.code:
Little's Law:
WIP = Throughput x Cycle Time   ->   Cycle Time = WIP / Throughput

Worked example:
WIP = 12 items, Throughput = 3 items/day
Cycle Time = 12 / 3 = 4 days

To halve cycle time to 2 days WITHOUT hiring:
2 = WIP / 3  ->  WIP = 6  (lower the WIP limit, don't work longer)

Per-ticket cycle time in working days (Excel):
=NETWORKDAYS(Start_date, Done_date)
lessons.tryit: Draw a Kanban board for your analytics requests with columns Backlog -> In Progress -> Review -> Done, and set a WIP limit for 'In Progress'. Log 10 recent tickets' start and done dates, compute each cycle time with =NETWORKDAYS(start, done), then find your 85th-percentile cycle time and use it to propose an SLA to stakeholders.

mistakes.m: Setting no WIP limit (or a very high one), so everything is 'in progress' and nothing finishes.
mistakes.fix: Start with a tight limit (roughly 1 to 1.5x the number of people), enforce 'stop starting, start finishing', and only raise it if flow is genuinely starved.

mistakes.m: Confusing cycle time with lead time.
mistakes.fix: Lead time is measured from the customer's request; cycle time from when work actually starts. Report both, and be explicit about which one your SLA promises.

mistakes.m: The analyst working outside the process, taking requests via random DMs.
mistakes.fix: Put analytics work on the shared board as sized items with acceptance criteria, and use a capped 'urgent' swimlane so ad-hoc requests are visible and traded off, not silently absorbed.

quiz.q: A Kanban board shows an average of 12 items in progress and the team completes 3 items per day. By Little's Law, what is the average cycle time?
opts: 36 days
4 days
0.25 days
15 days
quiz.why: Little's Law rearranges to cycle time = WIP / throughput = 12 / 3 = 4 days.
quiz.approach: Write WIP = throughput x cycle time, isolate cycle time as WIP/throughput, then divide.

quiz.q: Why does lowering the WIP limit often reduce cycle time?
opts: It forces people to work more hours per day
It reduces multitasking and queueing, so at the same throughput less WIP means shorter cycle time
It increases the number of items started at once
It only helps if the team also adds people to raise throughput
quiz.why: By Little's Law, with throughput roughly constant, cutting WIP directly lowers cycle time; practically, less multitasking and queueing lets items flow through faster — no extra staff required.
quiz.approach: Reason from cycle time = WIP/throughput: reduce the numerator while throughput holds, and cycle time falls.

quiz.q: How should a data analyst best integrate ad-hoc 'pull this number now' requests into an agile analytics team?
opts: Drop all planned work whenever any request arrives
Handle them invisibly via DMs so the board stays clean
Make them visible on the board in a capped 'urgent' swimlane so trade-offs are explicit
Refuse all ad-hoc requests outright
quiz.why: A visible, capped urgent swimlane keeps interrupt work transparent and bounded, so the team can consciously trade it off against planned work rather than being derailed or hiding it.
quiz.approach: Favour the option that preserves visibility and WIP discipline while still allowing genuine urgency — reject 'always drop everything', 'hide it', and 'refuse everything'.
